title: Wellbeing statistics: 2023 (updated) | Stats NZ
content:
## Wellbeing statistics: 2023 (updated)
25 September 2024, 10:45am
Wellbeing statistics give a picture of social wellbeing in the New Zealand population. They’re based on people’s assessments of their own lives, such as how satisfied they are, and objective information, such as their labour force status.
Wellbeing statistics: 2023 presents data from the 2023 General Social Survey (GSS).
About the data has high-level information about the GSS collection.
26 March 2025 : We have updated Wellbeing statistics: 2023 to include new data on digital inclusion and volunteering, as well as additional information on te reo Māori proficiency in Aotearoa New Zealand .
See the updated Excel file ‘Wellbeing statistics: 2023 (updated 26 March 2025)' for more data on te reo Māori. We have also added new Excel files with data on digital inclusion and volunteering. The CSV file has been updated to include all new data.
- Wellbeing statistics: 2023 (updated 26 March 2025) Excel spreadsheet, 1.4 MB

### Wellbeing statistics: 2023 – volunteering (added 26 March 2025)
Excel spreadsheet, 265 KB
Life satisfaction remains high People generally rate their family wellbeing highly Almost 2 in 5 people find it hard to meet everyday costs Housing affordability decreases for many People feel less safe Trust between people has decreased People generally comfortable having neighbours who are different from them Mental wellbeing lower among women than men Three-quarters of people felt they could be themselves in Aotearoa New Zealand Te reo Māori proficiency in Aotearoa New Zealand

### Life satisfaction remains high
People in Aotearoa New Zealand aged 15 years and over reported an average overall life satisfaction score of 7.6 out of 10 (where 0 is low and 10 is high), which is similar to the mean rating of 7.7 out of 10 in 2021.
In 2023, some groups rated their overall life satisfaction significantly lower, on average, than the total population. These included:
- families composed of one parent with child(ren) (7.0 out of 10)
- disabled people (based on the Washington Group Short Set – Enhanced questions), aged 15 to 64 years (6.3)
- people with a household income of $30,000 or less (7.2)
People reported the things they do in life are worthwhile, with a mean rating of 7.9 out of 10, which is similar to 8.0 in 2021.
Of those employed, 74 percent were satisfied or very satisfied with their job, and 69 percent were satisfied or very satisfied with their work/life balance.
In 2023, 84 percent of people expected to rate their life satisfaction as 7 or higher out of 10 in five years’ time, down from 87 percent in 2021.

### People generally rate their family wellbeing highly
When asked to rate their family wellbeing, the mean score given by people living in Aotearoa New Zealand was 7.6 out of 10, compared with 7.7 in 2021.
In 2023, the percentages of those rating their family wellbeing highly (at 7 or higher out of 10) differed by migrant status, including:
- 87 percent of recent migrants (people who arrived in New Zealand in the last five years)
- 81 percent of long-term migrants
- 76 percent of people born in Aotearoa New Zealand.

## Almost 2 in 5 people find it hard to meet everyday costs
In 2023, 39 percent of people reported they had only just enough, or not enough, money to meet everyday needs, similar to 37 percent in 2018.
For families composed of one parent with child(ren), this rate was 59 percent in 2023, which was similar to 61 percent in 2018.
Almost half (48 percent) of people reported they had gone without or cut back on fresh fruit or vegetables in 2023, up from 23 percent in 2018. For families composed of one parent with child(ren), this rate was 61 percent in 2023, up from 40 percent in 2018.
Percentage of people cutting costs, by measure taken, 2018 and 2023

Avoided visiting local places, including shops

Spent less on hobbies or other special interests

Put up with being cold

Delayed replacing or repairing broken or damaged appliances

Error bars represent variability in estimates and have been calculated using rounded numbers. ‘Measure taken’ refers to something done a little or a lot in the last 12 months to help cut costs.
Wellbeing statistics: 2018 data has been used for these comparisons because only limited data was published in 2021.

### Housing affordability decreases for many
People’s mean rating of the affordability of their house or flat fell to 6.1 in 2023 from 6.5 in 2021, on a scale from 0 (very unaffordable) to 10 (very affordable).
In 2023, 14 percent of people in Aotearoa New Zealand rated their homes as unaffordable (a score of between 0 and 3 out of 10), compared with 9.2 percent in 2021. For families composed of one parent with child(ren), this rate was 21 percent in 2023, compared with 17 percent in 2021.
In 2023, the mean rating for housing affordability of people who lived in an owner-occupied house was 6.2 out of 10, while people who lived in non-owner-occupied houses reported a lower mean rating of 5.8.
In 2023, 14 percent of owner-occupiers said their homes were ‘always’ or ‘often’ colder than they would like in winter. For non-owner-occupiers this proportion was 33 percent, and for families composed of one parent with child(ren), 29 percent.

### People feel less safe
People in Aotearoa New Zealand reported feeling less safe in 2023, compared with 2021. The proportion of those who felt safe or very safe when:
- walking alone in their neighbourhood after dark fell to 55 percent in 2023, from 59 percent in 2021
- being at home by themselves at night fell to 80 percent in 2023, from 85 percent in 2021.
In 2023, 16 percent of people said they had a crime committed against them in the last 12 months.
One in 5 people (22 percent) reported experiencing discrimination in the last 12 months, similar to 2021 (21 percent).

### Trust between people has decreased
On average, people rated their trust in most people in Aotearoa New Zealand at 6.5 in 2023 on a 0 to 10 scale, down from 6.7 in 2021. In addition to generalised trust, trust in institutions like the police and media also fell.
New Zealanders’ trust in key institutions declines has more information.

### People generally comfortable having neighbours who are different from them
In 2023, most people in Aotearoa New Zealand said they felt ‘comfortable’ or ‘very comfortable’ about a new neighbour with:
- a different religion (87 percent of people)
- a different sexual orientation (84 percent)
- a different ethnicity (89 percent)
- a different language (84 percent)
- a disability or long-term health condition (85 percent).
However, a lower proportion of people (55 percent) said they were comfortable or very comfortable about a new neighbour with a mental illness, compared with 58 percent in 2021.

### Mental wellbeing lower among women than men
The proportion of people in Aotearoa New Zealand who experienced poor mental wellbeing (based on the WHO-5 mental wellbeing index) was 26 percent in 2023, which is similar to 28 percent in 2021.
In 2023, 31 percent of women experienced poor mental wellbeing, compared with 22 percent of men.
Just over two-thirds of people (67 percent) said they would find it ‘easy’ or ‘very easy’ to talk to someone if they were feeling down or depressed, up from 62 percent in 2021.

### Three-quarters of people felt they could be themselves in Aotearoa New Zealand
The proportion of people who found it easy or very easy to be themselves (ability to express their identity) in Aotearoa New Zealand decreased to 76 percent in 2023, from 80 percent in 2021. Some groups rated their ability to express themselves significantly lower than the total population in 2023. These groups included:
- the LGBT+ population, where 64 percent of people found it easy or very easy to express themselves
- disabled people aged 15 to 64 years, where 59 percent of people found it easy or very easy to express themselves.

### Te reo Māori proficiency in Aotearoa New Zealand
Te reo Māori proficiency is asked in the context of how well people feel they can speak te reo Māori in everyday conversation, where ‘very well’ is defined as ‘I can talk about almost anything in te reo Māori’ and ‘well’ is defined as ‘I can talk about many things in te reo Māori’. In 2023:
- 2.3 percent of people reported they could speak te reo Māori well or very well, similar to 2.5 percent in 2021
- 11 percent of Māori reported they could speak te reo Māori well or very well, similar to 13 percent in 2021
- 2.9 percent of Pacific peoples reported they could speak te reo Māori well or very well.
For the same question, speaking te reo Māori ‘fairly well’ is defined as ‘l can talk about some things in te reo Māori’. In 2023:
- 3.9 percent of people reported they could speak te reo Māori fairly well, down from 5.2 percent in 2021
- 15 percent of Māori reported they could speak te reo Māori fairly well, compared with 19 percent in 2021
- 4.4 percent of Pacific peoples reported they could speak te reo Māori fairly well, compared with 10 percent in 2021 – the 2021 result has a high sampling error (+/- 5.8 percent), so should be treated with caution.
26 March 2025 : We’ve added the below key facts to highlight how te reo Māori is learnt and spoken across different groups in Aotearoa New Zealand.
- For the total population who could speak at least a few words or phrases of te reo Māori, the most common ways they learnt the language were: through media (44.2 percent) through primary or secondary school (42.6 percent) at work (38.7 percent).
- through media (44.2 percent)
- through primary or secondary school (42.6 percent)
- at work (38.7 percent).
- For Māori who could speak at least a few words or phrases of te reo Māori, the most common ways they learnt were: by listening and speaking with relatives, friends, or neighbours (51.8 percent) through primary or secondary school (46.3 percent) by listening and speaking at home (40.1 percent).
- by listening and speaking with relatives, friends, or neighbours (51.8 percent)
- through primary or secondary school (46.3 percent)
- by listening and speaking at home (40.1 percent).
- 79.3 percent of 15–24-year-olds who could speak at least a few words or phrases of te reo Māori learnt it through primary or secondary school (excluding kōhanga reo, kura kaupapa, or wharekura), compared with 11.8 percent of those aged 65 years and older who learnt this way.
- 33.3 percent of 25–44-year-olds who were able to speak at least a few words or phrases of te reo Māori used at least some te reo Māori when speaking to adults in their home.
- 10.8 percent of Māori spoke te reo Māori at least equally with English or another language when speaking to children in their home.

## Revised wellbeing statistics for 2021
We have republished the 2021 data and CSV files to correct a processing error. The issue only affects 2021 New Zealand General Social Survey (GSS) data.
Wellbeing statistics: 2021 has the revised data files.
Most estimates in the tables have been updated. However, all changes are small and well within sample errors, are not statistically significant, and do not change the interpretation of the data.
We apologise for this error and any inconvenience caused.
Wellbeing statistics: 2023 presents data from the 2023 General Social Survey. The data collection began on 15 May 2023 and finished on 21 April 2024.
For many of the questions, the respondents were asked to rate their experience on a scale of 0 to 10, where 0 is low and 10 is high. Other questions used metrics ranging from ‘very good’ or ‘satisfied’, to ‘very bad’ or ‘dissatisfied’, or something similar.

### Notes about disability status
References to disabled people in Wellbeing statistics: 2023 use disability status based on the Washington Group Short Set – Enhanced (WGSS-E) questions.
This includes the Washington Group Short Set (WGSS) of questions, which identifies disabled people as those who report having more than ‘some difficulty’ seeing, hearing, walking or climbing stairs, remembering or concentrating, communicating, or with self-care. These questions were used in 2016 GSS and 2018 GSS to identify disability. In 2021 and 2023, additional questions about upper-body functioning, dexterity, and feelings of anxiety and depression were included to create a disability status aligned with the WGSS-E questions.
The enhanced set of questions identify a broader group of disabled people than in previous GSS data collections. However, they still exclude some disabled people and are not a measure of disability prevalence. Disability status derived from these questions is used to estimate differences in the characteristics of disabled and non-disabled populations.
For comparability with 2016 and 2018, wellbeing estimates for disabled people using the WGSS of questions are included in the tables, available under Download data .
Wellbeing statistics are typically reported for the total population aged 15 years and over. However, the incidence of disability increases with age, which means older people are disproportionately represented in disability data. People aged 65 years and over also tend to have quite different wellbeing outcomes than younger people. Disability data has been limited to 15 to 64 years so that the comparisons between disabled people and non-disabled people are not affected by older people’s differing disability rates and wellbeing outcomes.
Mental wellbeing was measured using the World Health Organization’s WHO-5 mental wellbeing index, which covers five aspects of mental wellbeing, including how cheerful, calm, active, and rested people felt, and how much of their daily life was filled with interesting things.

## Definitions and metadata
New Zealand General Social Survey – DataInfo+ provides the definitions of terms used in this release and general methodology used to produce the wellbeing statistics.
GSS 2023 data collection – DataInfo+ provides information specific to 2023 General Social Survey.
Sandi Reily 021 285 9191 media@stats.govt.nz
